statement: Switzerland's relationship with the EU is governed by over 120 bilateral agreements negotiated between 1999 and 2024. After rejecting EEA membership in 1992 (50.3% No), Switzerland chose a bilateral path. The EU has increasingly criticised this approach as cherry-picking and demanded institutional framework agreements to govern the relationship.
source_name: Swiss Federal Council — European policy documentation
caveats: The Swiss bilateral model is sometimes cited as an alternative to EU membership, but the EU has signalled it will not extend this model to other countries. Switzerland's situation is also complicated by ongoing institutional framework negotiations.

related_entries.statement: The EEA Agreement covers the EU's four freedoms (goods, services, capital, persons) and flanking policies (competition, state aid, public procurement, company law, environment, social policy). It explicitly excludes the Common Agricultural Policy (CAP), Common Fisheries Policy (CFP), customs union, common trade policy, common foreign and security policy, and justice/home affairs.
